doc.title: Retire bonds early — gain or loss
doc.event: Two years after issuing {face} of {statedRateAnnual} bonds (market rate {marketRateAnnual} at issuance), and just after the fourth coupon, Ramos Co. buys the bonds back on the open market. Prepare the retirement entry.

variants.label: Bought back at 101 — above carrying value

entries.caption: Retire early at 101 (505,000) when carrying value is the period-4 balance

lines.label: face off the books
lines.why: The face leaves at face — the liability account never carried anything else.
lines.trap: Face comes off in full even in an early retirement; the discount handles the rest.

lines.label: price − carrying value
lines.why: You paid more than the debt was carried at — the excess is a loss, reported in income in the period of retirement.
lines.trap: The comparison is price vs CARRYING VALUE, never price vs face. Face ignores the unamortized discount and gets the gain/loss wrong every time.

lines.label: unamortized remainder
lines.why: Whatever discount hasn't amortized yet dies with the bond — credited away so the contra account zeroes out.
lines.trap: Forgetting this line leaves a phantom contra-liability on the books for a bond that no longer exists.

lines.label: reacquisition price
lines.why: The open-market price (often quoted as a percentage — 101 means 101% of face).
lines.trap: The quoted percentage applies to FACE: 101 on a 500,000 face = 505,000, regardless of carrying value.

computationPaths.narration: Loss = reacquisition price − carrying value. Carrying value = face − unamortized discount (read it straight off the schedule's period-4 row). Any accrued interest to the retirement date is settled in a separate entry first.

variants.label: Bought back at 94 — below carrying value

entries.caption: Retire early at 94 (470,000) when carrying value is the period-4 balance

lines.label: face off the books
lines.trap: Same debit whether the retirement produces a gain or a loss.

lines.label: unamortized remainder
lines.why: The remaining discount is cleared with the bond.
lines.trap: This credit exists in BOTH the gain and loss cases — it's about closing the contra account, not about the outcome.

lines.label: reacquisition price
lines.trap: Percentage of FACE, not of carrying value.

lines.label: carrying value − price
lines.why: Extinguishing debt for less than its book value is a gain — the plug that balances the entry, on the credit side this time.
lines.trap: Gain or loss is always the PLUG after face, remaining discount/premium, and cash are placed. If your plug lands on the wrong side, re-check the carrying value.

computationPaths.narration: Gain = carrying value − reacquisition price. Same three ingredients as the loss case — the sign of the difference just flips which side the plug lands on.

memorize.body: Gain/loss on early retirement = carrying value − reacquisition price. Positive → gain (credit); negative → loss (debit). Carrying value = face ± unamortized premium/discount, read off the schedule at the retirement date.

Four-line ritual: (1) Dr Bonds Payable at face, (2) clear the unamortized discount/premium, (3) Cr Cash at the quoted price, (4) plug the gain or loss. Do them in that order and the plug can't hide from you.
If retirement falls BETWEEN coupon dates, first bring amortization and accrued interest up to the retirement date — the carrying value you compare against must be current as of that day.

questions.prompt: Discount bonds with a period-4 carrying value are reacquired at 101 (505,000 on 500,000 face). What is the loss on redemption?

distractors.feedback: That's the UNAMORTIZED DISCOUNT — an ingredient of the entry, not the loss. The loss compares the price PAID with the carrying value.

traces.label: Schedule carrying value ↔ unamortized discount ↔ the plug

build.scaffold: Anchor on the carrying value at the retirement date (schedule, last column). Then: face off, remaining discount/premium off, cash at the quoted price, and the gain or loss is whatever balances.
